title: Symmetry-Protected Fast Relaxation and the Strong Quantum Mpemba Effect
authors: Zijun Wei, Mingdi Xu, Yefeng Song, Yangqian Yan, Lei Pan
abstract: Understanding how symmetry constrains dissipative relaxation in open quantum many-body systems remains a central challenge in nonequilibrium physics. Here we uncover a symmetry-selective Liouvillian mechanism that protects an isolated fast-decay channel in a long-range XXZ spin chain subject to dephasing noise. At the \(SU(2)\)-symmetric point, highly symmetric initial states couple exclusively to an exact Liouvillian eigenmode with decay rate \(λ=-2\), producing universal exponential relaxation independent of system size and interaction range. Breaking the symmetry restores overlap with slow Liouvillian modes and substantially suppresses the relaxation dynamics. This symmetry-filtered mode accessibility naturally gives rise to a strong quantum Mpemba effect, where a state farther from the steady state relaxes anomalously faster than closer thermal states. Our results establish symmetry-protected fast relaxation as a mechanism for controlling nonequilibrium pathways in open quantum systems.
summary: This paper identifies a mechanism where SU(2) symmetry protects a fast-decaying channel in a long-range XXZ spin chain. This protection causes a strong quantum Mpemba effect, where highly symmetric states relax much faster than less symmetric thermal states. The findings suggest that symmetry can be used to engineer and control relaxation dynamics in open quantum many-body systems.
main_problem: Understanding how symmetry constrains dissipative relaxation in open many-body systems and identifying the microscopic origin of the strong quantum Mpemba effect.
main_result: The authors discover a symmetry-protected fast-decay channel in an XXZ spin chain where SU(2) symmetry allows certain states to bypass slow decay modes, leading to an anomalous relaxation speed where states farther from equilibrium relax faster than closer ones.
method: Liouvillian spectral decomposition, symmetry analysis using SU(2) and permutation groups, and analytical derivation of exact Liouvillian eigenmodes.
model_or_system: A long-range spin-1/2 XXZ spin chain subject to local dephasing noise, described by a Lindblad master equation with an interaction exponent alpha.
key_observables: Trace distance (D(t)) from the steady state and the spectral weight/overlap of initial states with Liouvillian eigenmodes.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter (Delta), interaction range (alpha), and temperature (beta).
main_assumptions: The system is governed by a Markovian Lindblad master equation with local dephasing jump operators.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the mechanism showing how symmetric states couple to a single fast mode while symmetry-broken states project onto slow modes; Figure 2 shows the ground state relaxation dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of symmetry-constrained relaxation, presents the analytical identification of a fast-decay mode at the SU(2) point, explores the effect of symmetry breaking and interaction range, and concludes with the emergence of the strong quantum Mpemba effect.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental mechanism for controlling nonequilibrium pathways and many-body lifetimes in open quantum systems using symmetry as a control knob, which is highly relevant to the stability of states in trapped ions or Rydberg arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of symmetry-protected Liouvillian relaxation. Left: A highly symmetric initial state couples exclusively to an isolated fast-decay Liouvillian mode in the same symmetry sector, producing single-channel exponential relaxation. Right: A symmetry-broken initial state projects onto multiple Liouvillian sectors, including slow modes, leading to slower relaxation and a hierarchy of dissipative timescales. This symmetry-filtered mode accessibility underlies the strong quantum Mpemba effect discussed below.

FIG. 3. Emergence and disappearance of the strong quan- tum Mpemba effect. The dashed line denotes the universal symmetry-protected trajectory D(t) ∼e−2t of the ground state. Solid curves represent finite-temperature initial states for different interaction exponents α. For α > 0, the initially farther ground state relaxes faster than thermal states and overtakes them, demonstrating a strong quantum Mpemba effect. In the all-to-all limit (α = 0), the relaxation trajec- tories collapse because the enlarged permutation symmetry generates a degenerate Liouvillian manifold at λ = −2. Inset: Spectral overlap showing both T = 0 and finite-temperature states occupy the same degenerate λ = −2...

FIG. 2. Ground-state relaxation dynamics in the long-range XXZ model (L = 8, J = 1). (a) In the SU(2)-symmetric XXX limit (∆= 1), the relaxation dynamics collapse onto the universal exponential law D(t) ∼e−2t for all interaction exponents α. The inset shows that the ground state overlaps exclusively with Liouvillian modes at λ = −2, demonstrating symmetry-selected access to an isolated fast-decay channel. (b) Breaking the symmetry through anisotropy (∆= 1.1) fragments the Liouvillian spectrum and activates multiple de- cay sectors, including slow modes. The resulting slow-mode overlap suppresses the relaxation dynamics and produces a pronounced interaction-range-dependent slowdown.

FIG. 4. Interaction-range evolution and symmetry-breaking control of relaxation. The relaxation dynamics of the ground state (T = 0), thermal state (T = 10), Z2-symmetric state, and domain-wall state are shown for different interaction exponents: (a) α = 1, (b) α = 2, (c) α = 3, and (d) α = 4. The highly symmetric ground state remains dominated by the isolated fast-decay Liouvillian channel at λ = −2, whereas lower-symmetry states develop finite overlap with slow modes and therefore relax more slowly. The interaction range controls the separation between relaxation trajectories, while the hierarchy is fixed by symmetry-filtered Liouvillian accessibility.

FIG. 5. Matrix representation of the analytically constructed SU(2)-symmetric operator O(2) (left) and the numerically ob- tained Liouvillian eigenmode at λ = −2 (right). The identical structure confirms that the universal fast-relaxation channel is uniquely governed by the symmetry-protected mode O(2).
